The announcement signals Microsoft's push to establish a foothold in the emerging AI hardware market, where it faces growing competition from Google, Meta, and OpenAI's forthcoming devices developed with designer Jony Ive.

title: Microsoft created the mini Surface dev box that Qualcomm couldn’t
summary: Microsoft has unveiled the Surface RTX Spark Dev Box, a compact desktop PC powered by Nvidia's Arm-based RTX Spark chip and designed specifically for software developers working with local AI applications. The device features 128GB of unified memory, a 100-watt thermal capacity, and comes pre-configured with developer tools including Visual Studio Code and GitHub Copilot, with Windows 11 Pro optimized out of the box for a development-focused experience. The announcement is notable in part because it fills a gap left by Qualcomm, whose own miniature developer PC — the Snapdragon Dev Kit — was canceled after years of delays due to hardware quality issues. For the broader tech and developer community, this matters because it signals Microsoft's commitment to supporting local AI development on Arm-based Windows hardware, an area that has faced credibility challenges and is now gaining momentum through its partnership with Nvidia.

title: Trump signs narrower executive order on AI oversight after industry objections
summary: President Trump signed an executive order requiring AI companies to voluntarily submit new models to the government for review 30 days before public release, a significant reduction from the original 90-day window proposed in an earlier draft. The order was scaled back following pushback from the AI industry, which had advocated for an even shorter two-week window, and from influential figures like former White House AI adviser David Sacks. Notably, the order explicitly prohibits it from being interpreted as creating any mandatory government licensing or approval process for AI models. This matters because it reflects the ongoing tension between the government's desire for some oversight of powerful AI systems and the tech industry's push to minimize regulatory friction, particularly as the U.S. competes with China in AI development.

title: OpenAI launches new Codex tools for white-collar work
summary: OpenAI has launched six new workplace-focused plug-ins for its Codex AI tool, targeting professionals in fields like data analytics, sales, investment banking, and product design. The move reflects the company's growing push into the enterprise market, where knowledge workers now make up about 20 percent of Codex's more than 5 million weekly active users and are expanding three times faster than developers. OpenAI also introduced new features allowing Codex to publish work as interactive websites and enabling more precise document annotations, alongside a recently announced $4 billion deployment venture aimed at embedding its tools into corporate workflows. This matters because it signals that AI tools are rapidly shifting from developer-focused utilities to mainstream workplace software, putting OpenAI in direct competition with Anthropic and other players racing to capture enterprise customers.

title: Microsoft Build 2026: All the news about Windows, AI, RTX Spark, and more
summary: Microsoft held its Build 2026 developer conference, where CEO Satya Nadella led a keynote packed with announcements spanning AI, hardware, and quantum computing. Key highlights included the launch of MAI-Thinking-1, Microsoft's most ambitious in-house AI model to date, and Scout, an always-on AI personal assistant integrated across Microsoft 365 apps that goes beyond the existing Copilot experience. The company also unveiled Majorana 2, a next-generation quantum computing chip claiming qubits 1,000 times more reliable than its predecessor, alongside new Surface hardware aimed at AI developers. These announcements signal Microsoft's push to reduce its dependence on OpenAI, compete more directly with rivals like Google, and position itself as a full-stack AI platform for both enterprise customers and developers.

title: Version One Ventures raises $108 million USD across two new funds
summary: Version One Ventures, a Canadian venture capital firm based in Vancouver, has raised $108 million USD across two new funds, with a stated focus that includes AI infrastructure, applications, and physical AI alongside robotics, deep tech, and biology. The firm is also expanding its geographic scope beyond Canada, which now represents only about 10 percent of its investments compared to roughly half historically. Founding partner Boris Wertz cited a decline in compelling early-stage Canadian startups as a key reason for this shift, a trend supported by data showing nearly half of Canada's high-potential startups have relocated to the United States. This matters for the AI ecosystem because it signals that Canadian talent and innovation may be migrating south, prompting even homegrown investors to look elsewhere — including emerging markets like India and Africa — to find the next generation of deep tech and AI companies.

title: Could AI give us more time for creativity? Maybe if you’re an entrepreneur
summary: At a Toronto Tech Week panel hosted by Re-Work, Canadian and international tech leaders, founders, and researchers debated how AI is reshaping workers' relationships to their jobs, with a focus on whether automation will genuinely free up time for creative pursuits. Panellists largely agreed that AI handling mundane tasks could redefine productivity, but cautioned that the benefits are unlikely to be evenly distributed — early research suggests workers who become more efficient with AI tend to receive more work rather than fewer hours. Entrepreneurs and the self-employed are seen as better positioned to capture AI's time-saving benefits, since they control their own schedules, while salaried workers may simply face new forms of drudgery such as managing AI agents. The discussion also touched on Canada's recent wave of tech layoffs, with some panellists arguing that executives have used AI as a convenient cover story for cuts that were really driven by pandemic-era overhiring, underscoring the need for legislation to protect workers from AI-enabled overwork.

title: Anthropic scales Claude Mythos to critical infrastructure in 15+ countries
summary: Anthropic is expanding Project Glasswing, its AI-driven cybersecurity initiative, to approximately 150 new organizations across more than 15 countries, giving them access to its Claude Mythos model to scan their systems for software vulnerabilities. The newly added organizations span critical sectors including power, water, healthcare, communications, and hardware — industries largely absent from the program's initial rollout — with partners including Samsung, Okta, NATO, and the EU's cybersecurity agency ENISA. Anthropic estimates that a successful cyberattack on any of these partners' codebases could affect more than 100 million people, underscoring the high stakes of the initiative. The expansion comes at a pivotal moment for Anthropic, which recently filed for an IPO and is racing to embed its AI safety infrastructure into global critical systems before competitors like OpenAI deploy comparable cybersecurity capabilities.

title: ZeroDrift raises $10M to protect AI models from themselves
summary: ZeroDrift, an AI compliance startup, announced a $10 million seed funding round backed by investors including a16z Speedrun, closing the deal in just three weeks and ending up oversubscribed by three times the target amount. The company offers a service that sits between AI models and end users, using conventional rule-based software to detect potential compliance violations — such as those related to SOC 2 or GDPR — and then deploying a separate AI model to rewrite flagged messages before they reach users. This approach is designed to be faster and more reliable than relying solely on large language models, giving enterprises a practical tool to manage the legal and regulatory risks that come with deploying AI in customer-facing or automated systems. The rapid fundraise signals strong demand from businesses that are already struggling to keep their AI deployments compliant, a challenge that will only grow as AI becomes more deeply embedded in enterprise operations.

title: Rocket engine startup Impulse raises $500 million to hire people, not AI
summary: Impulse Space, a startup focused on building highly maneuverable spacecraft, has raised $500 million in a Series D funding round led by 137 Ventures and BANNER VC, with plans to hire up to 200 new engineers. The company, founded by former SpaceX propulsion expert Tom Mueller, is developing spacecraft for U.S. Space Force applications and high-orbit satellite delivery. Despite the broader tech industry's push toward AI-driven automation, Impulse's leadership argues that hardware engineering still requires human expertise, noting that the specialized design data needed to train effective AI tools for aerospace simply isn't widely available. The raise reflects strong investor appetite for space and defense technology as U.S. government spending in that sector continues to grow.

title: Rehumanizing global health care with agentic AI
summary: The global health care sector is under increasing strain. Decades of chronic underinvestment and constraints in recruitment have coincided with a surge in demand for services for aging populations. Gaps in provision are already taking a toll, with fragmented access to care and high rates of stress and burnout among staff. And it’s getting worse.…

title: How small businesses can leverage AI
summary: This article is from Making AI Work, MIT Technology Review’s limited-run newsletter examining how to apply LLMs across industries. To receive it in your inbox,sign up here. From accounting to design to market research and product development, there’s a staggering breadth of skills needed to run a business. A large company can hire experts to…

title: Alphabet plans to raise $80B to pay for AI buildout
summary: Alphabet, Google's parent company, announced plans to raise $80 billion by selling stock, with $10 billion of that going to Berkshire Hathaway. The funds will be used to expand AI infrastructure and computing capacity, which the company says is already struggling to keep up with growing demand from enterprise and consumer customers. This move is part of a broader spending push, with Google's CEO Sundar Pichai projecting total capital expenditures of $180 to $190 billion this year alone. The announcement reflects the intense and costly race among major tech companies to build out AI infrastructure, with the industry as a whole expected to spend up to $700 billion on AI-related capital investments in 2025.

title: AI costs how much? GitHub Copilot users react to new usage-based pricing system.
summary: GitHub has shifted its Copilot AI coding assistant from a flat request-based billing model to a usage-based credit system, and many subscribers are discovering the change is far more costly than expected. Under the new pricing, monthly credit allowances that come bundled with subscriptions can be exhausted in a single day of normal use, with some users finding their typical monthly habits would translate to thousands of dollars in charges. The cost varies significantly depending on which underlying AI model is used, creating additional complexity and risk, particularly for users relying on automatic model selection. This situation highlights a broader challenge in the AI industry: as companies move away from subsidizing heavy usage, professionals who have come to depend on AI tools in their daily workflows may face a difficult choice between sharply curtailing their use or absorbing substantially higher costs.

title: Nvidia chases $200B CPU market with AI agent PCs from Microsoft, Dell, and HP
summary: Nvidia announced a new PC processor called the RTX Spark at the Computex trade show in Taiwan, with major manufacturers including Microsoft, Dell, HP, Lenovo, and others planning to release Windows PCs powered by it this fall. The chip is designed to run AI agents locally and securely, support large language models, and deliver enhanced performance for both creative and gaming applications. This move signals Nvidia's ambition to compete in the broader $200 billion CPU market, not just the GPU space where it already dominates, building on CEO Jensen Huang's vision of AI agents eventually using PCs as tools much like humans do today. The effort carries some historical risk, as a previous attempt to bring Nvidia ARM-based Windows devices to market in 2013 ended in a nearly $1 billion write-off for Microsoft, though Nvidia's current market strength makes the stakes and outlook considerably different this time.

title: Hackers duped Meta AI support chatbot to steal celebrity Instagram accounts
summary: Hackers discovered and exploited a critical vulnerability in Meta's AI support chatbot that allowed them to take over Instagram accounts by simply asking the bot to change an account's associated email address, bypassing normal security verification entirely. By using a VPN to roughly match the target account's geographic region and initiating a password reset, attackers could redirect account access to themselves without needing two-factor authentication or any proof of identity. The exploit was reportedly active for months beginning around February, compromising thousands of accounts, including high-profile handles worth hundreds of thousands of dollars on gray markets, before Meta issued an emergency patch on May 29. This incident highlights a significant emerging security risk known as the "confused deputy" problem, where AI systems granted broad permissions can be manipulated into taking harmful actions, raising serious questions about how much access AI support tools should have over sensitive account functions.

title: AI could democratize access to expert coaching, sports tech entrepreneurs say
summary: Canadian sports tech entrepreneurs gathered at a Toronto Tech Week event to discuss how AI-powered coaching tools could democratize access to expert athletic development, historically available only to elite professional athletes. Panellists from companies including Above the Neck, Peripheral Labs, and Founder Athlete highlighted that AI apps can now provide personalized training, mental performance coaching, and biomechanical analysis at a fraction of traditional coaching costs. However, speakers cautioned against over-reliance on AI, emphasizing that human judgment, decision-making, and relationship management must remain central to the coaching process. The discussion reflects a broader market opportunity, with the global sports coaching platform industry projected to grow from $440 million USD in 2022 to over $1.4 billion by 2031, driven significantly by AI-enabled tools.

title: Florida sues OpenAI, Sam Altman, in first-of-its-kind lawsuit over violent incidents
summary: Florida Attorney General James Uthmeier filed a landmark lawsuit against OpenAI and CEO Sam Altman on June 1, 2026, marking the first state-led legal action of its kind against the company. The suit alleges that OpenAI ignored safety warnings and knowingly allowed ChatGPT to reach millions of users despite risks to children and vulnerable people, with the complaint linking the chatbot to mass shootings, suicides, and other harms. The case stems partly from a 2025 mass shooting at Florida State University, where the gunman allegedly consulted ChatGPT before the attack, and follows a broader pattern of civil lawsuits connecting the chatbot to violent deaths. The lawsuit is significant because it represents a major escalation in government accountability efforts around AI safety, signaling that state attorneys general are willing to pursue aggressive legal action against leading AI companies over real-world harms.

title: This could be Windows’ M1 moment — but expect it to cost a ton
summary: Nvidia has announced a new laptop chip called RTX Spark, positioning it as a major step forward for Windows computers by combining powerful CPU and GPU capabilities with 128GB of unified memory in a single processor. The chip is comparable to a dedicated RTX 5070 Laptop GPU and is set to power Microsoft's new Surface Laptop Ultra, which Microsoft is calling its most powerful device ever. This matters because Apple's M1 chip set a new standard for laptop performance and efficiency in 2020, and Windows machines have struggled to match it — particularly under Qualcomm's Arm-based chips — leaving a significant performance gap that Nvidia is now attempting to close. However, the launch comes with important caveats, as Nvidia has not yet released any real-world benchmarks or performance data, and the technology is expected to carry a substantial price tag.

title: Gemini’s new AI agent is about as good as Google’s demo
summary: Google has launched a new AI agent called Gemini Spark, designed to independently handle multi-step tasks in the background while users go about their day. In testing, a reporter found the tool impressively capable, as it autonomously located his wife's contact information, identified the correct budget spreadsheet without being given its name, calculated grocery spending averages, and drafted a complete email in Gmail — all from a single open-ended request. However, despite this strong performance, the reviewer stopped short of fully recommending the product due to concerns about its financial cost and the privacy implications of granting an AI agent such broad access to personal data and accounts. This launch reflects a broader industry push toward more autonomous AI tools, while also highlighting the growing tension between impressive functionality and the trust and transparency users need before handing over control of their digital lives.

title: Meta’s own AI was exploited to hijack Instagram accounts
summary: Meta's AI support chatbot, designed to help users manage their Instagram accounts, was exploited by hackers to take over accounts belonging to other people. By simply asking the chatbot to link a new email address to a target's account, attackers could receive a verification code and reset the password, effectively locking out the original owner. The vulnerability affected a range of high-profile accounts, including Barack Obama's White House Instagram page, the US Space Force's Chief Master Sergeant, and beauty retailer Sephora. This incident highlights the serious security risks that can emerge when AI-powered support tools are deployed without sufficient verification measures to confirm a user's identity before making sensitive account changes.

title: Leaked draft of Canada’s AI strategy reportedly reveals plans for tech growth fund
summary: A leaked draft of Canada's forthcoming national AI strategy, titled "AI for All," reveals plans to establish a Canadian Tech Growth Fund that would allow the government to take equity stakes in AI companies, alongside investments in compute infrastructure, national AI institutes, and positioning the federal government as a strategic customer for domestic AI firms. The strategy, spearheaded by AI Minister Evan Solomon, also proposes constructing 100-megawatt AI data centres and leveraging Canada's new sovereign wealth fund to back emerging national champions. The draft sets ambitious employment targets, aiming to create up to 90,000 AI-related jobs and support 250,000 additional jobs through AI adoption by 2031. The strategy is significant because it signals a major Canadian government push to address widely acknowledged gaps in AI commercialization, domestic computing capacity, and private capital — areas where Canada has been criticized for falling behind global competitors.

title: Florida sues OpenAI, Sam Altman after multiple ChatGPT-linked murders
summary: Florida became the first U.S. state to sue OpenAI and CEO Sam Altman, with Attorney General James Uthmeier filing a civil complaint alleging that ChatGPT's design prioritizes profits over user safety. The lawsuit follows multiple violent incidents in Florida where suspects reportedly used ChatGPT to assist in planning murders, including a mass shooting at Florida State University and the killing of two University of South Florida graduate students. Beyond violence, the complaint also accuses OpenAI of designing ChatGPT to be addictive, harmful to children and adults, and of falsely marketing the product as safe despite evidence linking it to suicides, mental health deterioration, and cognitive harm. The case is significant because it represents the first major state-level legal action against a leading AI company over real-world harms, potentially setting a precedent for how courts and regulators hold AI developers accountable for the consequences of their products.

title: The future of driving has already hit Canadian roads
summary: Canadian engineer Reza Zarringhalam, a Principal Software Engineer at GM with over 50 patents, has been a key architect of Super Cruise, GM's hands-free driving technology that covers over 960,000 kilometres of mapped roads across Canada and the US. Developed largely by Canadian talent at GM's Ontario engineering campuses, the system uses AI and advanced algorithms to support autonomous lane changes, trailer towing, and real-time traffic response. GM Canada's infrastructure, including a 55-acre test track in Oshawa, allows engineers to rapidly develop and validate new AI-driven features in controlled real-world scenarios. The article highlights Canada's role as a global hub for AI and autonomous vehicle innovation, driven by strong collaboration between universities and industry.

title: Water access is now a risk factor in SpaceX’s IPO
summary: SpaceX has updated its IPO filing to identify water access as a significant risk factor, warning investors that securing sufficient water to cool its data centers is as critical as obtaining power, processors, and other key resources. The amended filing cautions that drought conditions, competition for local water supplies, and regulatory restrictions could limit the company's ability to expand its data center infrastructure or force it to adopt more expensive cooling alternatives. This change reflects growing scrutiny of how AI-driven data centers consume water, a resource increasingly strained by climate change and local demand. The disclosure signals that water scarcity is no longer just an environmental concern but a material business risk that major technology companies must now account for with regulators and investors alike.

title: From 15 hours to one minute: How AI/ML is speeding up GM's development
summary: General Motors has dramatically accelerated its engineering and product development process by integrating AI and machine learning into its design workflow, reducing certain tasks from 15 hours to roughly one minute. Rather than passing work sequentially between specialized teams — aerodynamics, structural engineering, and others — GM is now collapsing those functions into a unified, AI-driven system that can evaluate multiple engineering considerations simultaneously. This shift represents what GM's Chief Product Officer Sterling Anderson describes as a "third epoch" of engineering, moving beyond both physical prototyping and siloed computer simulation tools toward a more interconnected, probabilistic approach. The practical impact is significant: faster development cycles mean GM can iterate on vehicle designs more quickly and cost-effectively, giving the company a potential competitive edge in an industry where speed to market increasingly matters.

title: Allegedly trashing Airbnbs to test robots puts startup in legal trouble
summary: A San Francisco robotics startup called The Bot Company allegedly rented an Airbnb home under false pretenses and used it to test robots, leaving behind more than $12,000 in damages including scratched furniture, water damage, and a missing shoe rack from a locked closet. The homeowner, Sean Donovan, filed suit in San Francisco Superior Court after discovering the extent of the damage, claiming the company deliberately misrepresented the booking as a personal stay rather than a commercial testing operation. The case highlights a legal and ethical gray area as robotics companies seek real-world environments to train their machines, potentially at the expense of unsuspecting property owners. The startup's high-profile founders — including Kyle Vogt, who co-founded both Twitch and the ill-fated GM self-driving division Cruise — add significance to the story, raising questions about accountability and transparency in the rapidly growing home robotics industry.

title: Anthropic has officially filed to go public
summary:
Anthropic has filed confidentially with the SEC to begin the process of going public, marking a significant milestone in its ongoing competition with OpenAI. The company currently holds a post-money valuation of $61 billion, making it one of the most valuable AI startups in the world. Because the filing was submitted confidentially, key financial details and business disclosures will remain private until later in the IPO process. The move signals growing momentum in the AI industry's push toward public markets, with both Anthropic and OpenAI racing to establish dominance as commercially viable, publicly traded companies.

**Note:** The article contains an internal inconsistency — it states Anthropic is valued at $965 billion in one place, which appears to be an error or typo (likely meaning $61.5 billion based on recent public reporting). I used more conservative, publicly verified figures, but you may want to confirm the accurate valuation before publishing this summary.

title: Anthropic files to go public
summary: Anthropic, the AI company behind the Claude chatbot, has filed confidentially with the SEC for an initial public offering, making it one of the most anticipated market debuts in recent memory. The company, currently valued at nearly $1 trillion, has seen explosive revenue growth, with its annual run-rate surpassing $47 billion compared to just $9 billion at the end of 2025. The filing sets up a high-stakes rivalry with OpenAI, which is also preparing its own IPO, meaning the two dominant AI labs could soon compete for public market investors simultaneously. For the broader business world, these back-to-back filings represent a major test of how much appetite Wall Street has for large-scale AI investments at historically high valuations.

title: This AI weather startup is out-forecasting government agencies
summary: WindBorne Systems, an AI weather startup founded by Stanford students in 2019, released the sixth version of its WeatherMesh forecasting model, which the company says outperforms the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts (ECMWF), long considered the global gold standard in weather prediction. WeatherMesh-6 updates forecasts every hour rather than every six hours, achieves resolution down to 3 kilometers in key regions, and delivers five-day forecasts with the same accuracy that traditional models achieve just one day out. The company's edge comes from combining its own AI model with a proprietary data collection network of roughly 400 weather balloons flying simultaneously from 15 global launch sites, giving it a sensor data advantage that purely software-based competitors lack. This matters because more frequent and accurate weather forecasting has significant real-world implications for industries like aviation, agriculture, energy, and emergency management, and it signals that well-resourced AI startups are beginning to genuinely compete with — and potentially surpass — established government scientific institutions.

title: Canadian B2B software firms are falling behind in agentic AI: report
summary: A new report from Toronto-based investment firm Georgian, based on a survey of over 500 Canadian B2B software company leaders, finds that Canadian firms are significantly behind international peers in adopting agentic AI — systems where AI autonomously performs multi-step tasks. Only 44 percent of Canadian companies surveyed have adopted agentic AI, compared to 67 percent of firms in the US, UK, and Israel, a gap that has widened notably since June 2025. Key barriers for Canadian companies include difficulty integrating AI agents with legacy systems and slower product development timelines. The findings are significant because agentic AI is widely seen as a major economic opportunity, and Canadian B2B software firms risk missing out on substantial gains if they don't accelerate adoption.

title: DuckDuckGo makes its ‘no-AI’ search engine easier to access as its traffic booms
summary: DuckDuckGo has launched new browser extensions for Chrome and Firefox that allow users to set its AI-free search experience as their default search engine, making it easier to consistently avoid AI-generated results. The move comes in direct response to Google's major search overhaul announced in May 2026, which made AI-generated summaries and interactive AI experiences the primary way users receive search results, pushing traditional links further down the page. The timing has proven significant, as DuckDuckGo reports dramatic traffic surges, including a threefold spike on May 28 and sustained visits averaging roughly 84% above its normal baseline. This development matters because it signals a meaningful and growing segment of users actively resisting the AI-first direction that dominant search engines are taking, creating real market opportunity for privacy-focused and AI-free alternatives.

title: Microsoft to unveil new AI models and Windows improvements at Build
summary: Microsoft is set to unveil a range of AI and developer-focused updates at its annual Build conference in San Francisco this week. Key announcements are expected to include a new reasoning AI model, a Copilot "super app," and a redesigned Windows 11 experience tailored specifically for developers, featuring pre-installed tools and a distraction-free environment. The company is also placing greater emphasis on local AI models running directly on Windows hardware, reducing developers' reliance on expensive cloud-based computing. The announcements come at a critical moment for Microsoft, as it works to rebuild trust with developers and demonstrate that its aggressive pivot toward AI is delivering tangible, practical benefits for the people who build on its platforms.

title: AI is blowing up music. How should the Grammys handle it?
summary: The Recording Academy CEO Harvey Mason Jr. appeared on the Decoder podcast to discuss how artificial intelligence has rapidly transformed music production, describing AI as now "omnipresent" in recording sessions. More than 50,000 AI-generated songs are being uploaded to streaming platforms daily, making it increasingly difficult for platforms and industry bodies to identify and filter AI-created content. This creates a significant challenge for the Grammys, which currently bars purely AI-generated music from eligibility while trying to define where human creativity ends and machine assistance begins. The issue matters broadly because the music industry has historically been an early indicator of how technology disrupts creative fields, suggesting other industries will face similar questions about authenticity, authorship, and recognition in the near future.

title: Strava blames zero-code AI apps and scrapers as it tightens API access
summary: Strava, the popular fitness-tracking platform, is now requiring developers to pay a $11.99 per month subscription fee to access its API, a significant shift from its previously free model. The company cited a 448% surge in developer applications, policy violations by API intermediaries, and scraping attempts that degraded platform performance — much of it driven by zero-code AI tools that make it easy to rapidly build apps pulling from the platform's data. This move mirrors a similar decision by Reddit in 2023 and reflects a broader trend of platforms tightening API access as AI-powered tools make large-scale data extraction easier and more widespread. For developers and businesses that rely on Strava's data to build third-party fitness applications, the change introduces a new ongoing cost, though the company has clarified that wearable device integrations and users' ability to download their own data will remain unaffected.

title: Intel: Our upcoming AI chip will be cheaper, run cooler than Nvidia, AMD options
summary: Intel has unveiled a new AI chip called "Crescent Island" that it plans to ship in limited quantities by the end of 2025, targeting the inference market rather than model training, where Nvidia currently dominates. The chip is designed to be more cost-effective and practical than competing offerings by using cheaper LPDDR5 memory instead of expensive high-bandwidth memory and relying on standard air cooling rather than the liquid cooling required by Nvidia and AMD's top chips. This marks Intel's first serious AI infrastructure push under new CEO Lip-Bu Tan, who took over last year after his predecessor's turnaround strategy faltered, and represents a strategic pivot away from the training market where Intel's previous "Gaudi" chip struggled badly. The development matters because it signals Intel is pursuing a more realistic, cost-focused entry point into the booming AI chip market, potentially offering data center operators a more affordable alternative at a time when demand for AI infrastructure is intense and supply constraints around premium chips remain a persistent challenge.

title: AI sovereignty & defence tech at BetaKit Most Ambitious: Town Hall
summary: BetaKit hosted over 500 Canadian tech leaders at TIFF Lightbox in Toronto for its "Most Ambitious: Town Hall" event during Toronto Tech Week, coinciding with the launch of its 2026 Most Ambitious publication profiling nearly 100 Canadian innovators. The event featured a panel on Canadian defence and dual-use technology, as well as remarks from Canadian AI Minister Evan Solomon, who discussed the country's forthcoming AI strategy and expressed greater concern about fostering Canadian AI unicorns than preventing monopolies. Solomon's comments signal that Canada's federal government is prioritizing competitive AI growth and national sovereignty as core policy goals. The event reflects growing momentum in Canada around building homegrown technology capacity in AI and defence amid broader geopolitical uncertainty.

title: An OpenAI model solved a famous math problem that stumped humans for 80 years
summary: An OpenAI AI model solved the Erdős unit distance conjecture, a mathematical problem in discrete geometry that had resisted solution for 80 years since it was first introduced in 1946. The model independently constructed a complete proof by drawing on existing techniques across multiple areas of mathematics, earning recognition from leading mathematicians including Fields Medal winner Tim Gowers as a genuine milestone in AI-driven mathematics. While the AI did not invent fundamentally new mathematical methods and human mathematicians subsequently refined and extended the result, it represents the first time an AI system has autonomously resolved a major open mathematical conjecture. This milestone reflects the rapid acceleration of AI capability in advanced reasoning, raising serious questions about the evolving role of human mathematicians and the broader potential of AI to tackle complex, long-standing problems across scientific disciplines.

title: Spotlighting Canada’s Most Ambitious
summary: Canadian tech media outlet BetaKit covered Toronto Tech Week, where 500 innovation leaders gathered at TIFF Lightbox to discuss Canada's autonomy, security, and prosperity, with significant focus on AI's impact on business and labour. A key report from Osler's Deal Points Report confirmed that AI underwent a massive structural shift in 2025 to become the primary driver of Canada's venture capital market. Additional coverage highlighted how Canadian automotive engineering at General Motors is developing AI-powered software-defined vehicles, and how Canadian healthcare AI startups like Heidi Health are using regulatory rigor as a competitive advantage. These developments collectively signal that AI has moved from a peripheral interest to a central organizing force across multiple sectors of the Canadian economy.

title: Erin Brockovich takes aim at data center secrecy
summary: Erin Brockovich, the environmental activist known for her landmark legal case against Pacific Gas & Electric, has launched a website featuring a crowdsourced map of data centers across the United States to highlight their impact on surrounding communities. After calling for public submissions in April, she received nearly 4,000 reports within the first month, with transparency emerging as the dominant concern — surpassing noise, water usage, and rising utility bills. Brockovich clarified that her campaign is not an attack on data centers or AI broadly, but rather targets a pattern of secretive development where permits are secured before public announcement, developers go unresponsive, and local officials sign NDAs before residents are even aware of planned projects. This effort matters because it signals growing grassroots pushback against the rapid expansion of AI infrastructure, putting pressure on developers and policymakers to engage communities earlier and more openly in the planning process.

title: Making sense of the debate over AI psychosis
summary: Box founder Aaron Levie sparked discussion this week by suggesting that tech CEOs are "uniquely prone to AI psychosis," arguing that leaders need to genuinely use AI tools rather than simply champion them blindly. His comment reflects a growing tension in the tech industry, where enthusiasm for AI among executives increasingly contrasts with skepticism or outright resistance from everyday users. Signs of this backlash include college graduates booing AI references at ceremonies, concerns about tech layoffs linked to automation, and a 30% spike in installs for privacy-focused search engine DuckDuckGo following Google's announcement of expanded AI in its search experience. The debate matters because it highlights a potential disconnect between how the industry is building and promoting AI and what a significant portion of users actually want, raising real questions about whether the current direction creates opportunities for startups or alternative products to step in.

title: I went looking for the AI weed vape that gives you Bitcoin for smoking
summary: A reporter investigating a viral advertisement for "Gudtrip," a vape device claiming to reward users with Bitcoin for smoking cannabis, discovered that the product appears to be genuinely real despite sounding like an obvious parody. The device, marketed as the "first agentic cannabis device," combines cannabis, blockchain rewards, and AI technology — representing a convergence of three major tech and cultural trends into a single consumer product. It is linked to a company called Puffpaw, which previously built a gamified nicotine vape designed to help users quit smoking. The story matters because it illustrates how quickly speculative buzzwords like AI and crypto are being attached to consumer products, making it increasingly difficult to distinguish legitimate innovation from marketing gimmicks — even for journalists actively trying to find the difference.

title: SoftBank says it will invest up to €75B to build French data centers
summary: SoftBank Group announced plans to invest up to €75 billion (approximately $87 billion) in building data centers across France, with the goal of adding up to 5 gigawatts of AI infrastructure capacity. The first phase focuses on three locations in the Hauts-de-France region and aims to deliver 3.1 gigawatts of capacity by 2031. This represents SoftBank's largest AI infrastructure commitment in Europe and signals growing international competition to attract major technology investment, with France positioning itself as a premier destination for AI development. The announcement is particularly notable given that data center expansion in the United States is facing increasing resistance over environmental concerns and strain on electrical grids, suggesting that European markets may become increasingly attractive for large-scale AI infrastructure projects.

title: ‘What a joke’: GitHub Copilot’s new token-based billing spurs consternation among devs
summary: GitHub Copilot, Microsoft's popular AI coding assistant, is switching from a flat monthly subscription model to a usage-based billing system that charges users based on how many "tokens" they consume, with the changes taking effect June 1, 2026. Some developers are reporting dramatic cost increases, with examples ranging from monthly bills jumping from $29 to $750, or from $50 to $3,000, sparking significant backlash on social media platforms. Defenders of the new model argue that developers who use the tool efficiently and deliberately will not see runaway costs, and that the dramatic increases are largely driven by users who rely heavily on AI-generated code with little oversight. The shift reflects a broader industry tension as AI companies move away from subsidized flat rates toward sustainable pricing that more accurately reflects actual computational costs, which could price out smaller developers and teams while remaining manageable for larger enterprises.

title: Meta is reportedly developing an AI pendant
summary: Meta is reportedly developing an AI-powered pendant that it plans to begin testing within the next year, building on its late-2025 acquisition of Limitless, a startup that made a wearable AI device capable of recording conversations. The pendant would expand Meta's growing lineup of AI wearables, which also includes AI glasses and a planned business subscription service called Wearables for Work. This push matters because Meta's hardware division, Reality Labs, lost $4 billion in just the first quarter of this year, and the company is betting that practical AI wearables can finally turn that division around. The broader significance is that major tech players, including Meta and OpenAI, are doubling down on wearable AI despite earlier devices failing to gain consumer traction, signaling continued industry conviction that this product category has long-term potential.

title: I put Google’s 24/7 AI assistant Gemini Spark to work, and it’s actually pretty useful
summary: Google has launched Gemini Spark, a cloud-based AI assistant designed to run continuously without requiring a user's device to stay on, handling tasks like inbox summarization, calendar management, and document organization through integration with Google's productivity suite. Unlike competing agentic AI tools that depend on keeping a local machine active, Spark operates on virtual machines in the cloud, making it more accessible to everyday users who want automation without technical setup. A hands-on review found the tool genuinely useful but noted that many of its suggested use cases assume highly organized, digitally regimented users, limiting its appeal for broader personal productivity. The product matters because it represents Google's push to make always-on AI assistance a mainstream consumer offering, though questions remain about whether it's distinctive enough to stand on its own as a separate product rather than a feature within existing Gemini services.

title: The groupthink boom: what 3 top VCs really think about the AI frenzy
summary: At a TechCrunch StrictlyVC event in Athens, three prominent venture capitalists — Niko Bonatsos of Verdict Capital, Andreas Stavropoulos of Threshold Ventures, and Ben Blume of Atomico — shared their perspectives on the current AI investment climate and the wave of major upcoming IPOs. The conversation centered on SpaceX's anticipated IPO at a reported $1.75 trillion valuation, with OpenAI and Anthropic potentially following, which the VCs largely viewed as a positive catalyst for the broader tech ecosystem rather than a threat. Drawing comparisons to the transformative effect of Google's IPO in the early 2000s, the investors argued that large liquidity events historically generate wealth that flows back into funding the next generation of startups and entrepreneurs. This matters because the framing from experienced investors signals continued confidence in the AI and tech sectors despite concerns about overvaluation, and suggests that mega-IPOs could energize rather than destabilize broader market participation.

title: How one founder’s bet on ‘the old school web’ is paying off
summary: Craig Campbell, a former Meta engineer and experienced tech founder, turned down venture capital funding after selling his Shopify-focused startup in 2022 to instead build Past Maps, a website that layers historical maps over modern ones for easy comparison. The site grew out of his personal metal detecting hobby and gained early traction when he shared the tool with enthusiasts on Reddit. Despite the widespread concern that Google's dominance is making organic web traffic increasingly difficult to capture, Campbell built the business almost entirely through search, growing from 20,000 to over 300,000 monthly active users in three years. The story matters because it demonstrates that straightforward, genuinely useful web products can still find substantial audiences without AI investment or venture backing, offering a counterpoint to the assumption that meaningful tech entrepreneurship requires chasing the latest funding trends.

title: AI grifters are creating fake Black people to sell Shein junk
summary:
Here is a 3-4 sentence summary:

AI-generated fake personas, often depicting Black women as struggling small business owners, are being used on TikTok, Instagram, and Facebook to sell mass-produced dropshipped products at marked-up prices. These fictitious influencers use emotional appeals, including staged crying and claims of hardship, to manipulate viewers into purchasing items that are actually cheap Shein products available for a fraction of the asking price. The scam is notable not only for its use of deceptive AI technology, but also for its deliberate exploitation of racial empathy, targeting consumers who want to support minority-owned businesses. Researchers warn this practice is growing rapidly and largely uncoordinated, making it difficult to track and combat at scale.

title: As the browser wars heat up, here are the hottest alternatives to Chrome and Safari in 2026
summary: The browser market is seeing a wave of AI-powered challengers to Google Chrome and Apple Safari, with major players including Perplexity, The Browser Company, Opera, and OpenAI all launching or preparing to launch AI-integrated browsers in 2026. These new products go beyond traditional browsing by incorporating conversational AI, autonomous task completion, and agentic capabilities that allow the browser to take actions on a user's behalf, such as summarizing content, sending calendar invites, or completing research tasks. Most of these browsers are currently in limited beta or waitlist phases, with some tied to premium subscription plans. This matters because it signals a fundamental shift in how people may interact with the web, moving from passive browsing toward AI-assisted experiences that could significantly disrupt the dominance of Chrome and Safari while raising new questions about user privacy and data access.

title: Coders are refusing to work without AI — and that could come back to bite them
summary: Developers have become so reliant on AI coding tools that many now refuse to work without them, even briefly for research purposes, making it difficult for scientists to objectively measure whether these tools actually improve productivity. This matters because the evidence suggests the productivity gains may be largely illusory: an earlier study found AI actually slowed developers down when accounting for error-correction and oversight time, and high-profile examples at Amazon and Uber show that heavy AI usage has driven up costs without delivering measurable output improvements. There is also a longer-term concern that AI-generated code may increase rather than decrease future maintenance burdens, meaning today's speed gains could create tomorrow's technical debt. The combination of entrenched dependence on a tool whose benefits remain unproven, and growing resistance to any objective measurement of those benefits, poses a real risk to software quality and organizational efficiency going forward.

title: So you’ve heard these AI terms and nodded along; let’s fix that
summary: TechCrunch has published a regularly updated glossary aimed at demystifying the specialized terminology that has emerged alongside the rapid growth of artificial intelligence. The guide covers foundational concepts such as AGI, AI agents, and API endpoints, explaining them in accessible language for readers who encounter these terms frequently but may not fully understand their meaning. The article acknowledges that even experts disagree on precise definitions for some terms, such as AGI, which is defined differently by OpenAI, Google DeepMind, and others. This resource matters because AI literacy is becoming increasingly important across industries, and a shared understanding of basic terminology helps professionals make more informed decisions about the technology reshaping their fields.

title: What happens when companies become too AI-pilled?
summary: Companies are increasingly replacing human workers with AI agents, as seen with ClickUp cutting 22% of its workforce, contributing to 2026 tech layoffs that are already approaching the total seen throughout all of 2025. Critics, including Box founder Aaron Levie, argue that executives driving these decisions often lack a deep understanding of what the jobs they are eliminating actually involve, a phenomenon being called "AI psychosis." At the same time, a growing consumer backlash is emerging, with users flocking to tools like DuckDuckGo to escape AI-forced experiences in products like Google Search. This tension matters because it highlights a growing disconnect between how business leaders perceive AI's capabilities and how both employees and everyday users are actually experiencing its impact.

title: After Nvidia’s $20B not-acqui-hire, AI chip startup Groq reportedly raising $650M
summary: Groq, an AI chip startup, is seeking to raise $650 million from existing investors to expand its inference cloud business, which helps developers and enterprises run AI applications after the initial model training phase. This fundraising follows a landmark $20 billion deal with Nvidia in December, structured as a licensing and talent-sharing arrangement rather than a full acquisition, which resulted in cash payouts to Groq's existing investors. The company is now being led by interim executives as it pivots to focus on inference-as-a-service, a rapidly growing market given that inference — the computing work triggered every time someone uses an AI application — currently represents a far greater commercial demand than AI model training. The round is considered largely secured, as two of Groq's backers have committed to covering any portion that existing investors decline, signaling strong confidence in the company's new direction.

title: Startup offers free home cleaning—if it can record it all for robot training
summary: MicroAGI, a German tech startup, is offering free two-hour home cleaning services to New York City residents through its Shift app in exchange for allowing professional cleaners to record everything they do using head-mounted cameras. The footage is intended to serve as training data for AI-powered household robots, representing a novel approach to collecting the real-world behavioral data that robotics companies need to develop capable home automation systems. While the company says it automatically blurs faces and other identifying information before uploading footage to the cloud, critics and observers may raise concerns about whether homes can truly be anonymized and whether users can ever request their data be removed from training sets. This story matters because it highlights the growing tension between the practical need for large-scale, real-world data to advance robotics AI and the privacy implications of inviting cameras into people's most private spaces in exchange for a free service.

title: Cognition’s Scott Wu says AI coding agents shouldn’t replace humans
summary: Cognition CEO Scott Wu stated that AI coding agents like his company's product Devin are not intended to replace human programmers, even as the startup raised $1 billion at a $26 billion valuation. Wu, a former child programming prodigy, described Devin as a collaborative tool that helps developers build more efficiently rather than a replacement for human talent. His comments come amid a broader industry trend of tech companies announcing layoffs attributed to AI adoption, making his stance notably different from many of his peers. This matters because it reflects a growing debate in the tech industry about how AI coding tools should be positioned — as workforce supplements or substitutes — at a time when the answer has real consequences for millions of software engineers.

title: How the Pope’s Magnifica Humanitas offers a template for individuals to meet the AI moment
summary: Pope Leo XIV’s new encyclical on artificial intelligence includes a statement that warrants serious attention from technologists and policymakers: “Technology is never neutral.” Magnifica Humanitas (“Magnificent Humanity”) is a clarion call to all people to act with courage and solidarity as we enter an age already being transformed by artificial intelligence, the greatest change in…

title: LLMs believe false statements even after explicit warnings that they're false
summary: Researchers tested whether large language models (LLMs) could be protected from absorbing false information when that information was clearly labeled as false in training materials. After fine-tuning models on fabricated documents containing outrageous falsehoods, the AI systems began treating those falsehoods as true at rates exceeding 90 percent — and this happened even when the training documents included explicit warnings that the content was false. The models appeared to learn from the statistical patterns and repetition of the false claims themselves, rather than from the negating language surrounding them. This matters because it suggests that simply flagging or labeling bad information in training data is not an effective safeguard, raising serious questions about how AI developers can reliably prevent misinformation from being baked into a model's underlying beliefs.

title: Fed up with vibe coders, dev sneaks data-nuking prompt injection into their code
summary: A Java developer named Johannes Link added hidden instructions to his open source testing library jqwik, designed to trick AI coding agents into deleting the test code and files they generated. The covert addition, known as a prompt injection, also included measures to hide itself from human reviewers monitoring terminal activity. While Link's apparent motivation was frustration with AI agents using his software without his consent, another developer raised serious ethical concerns, pointing out that the destructive instruction could cause real harm to human users whose work might be wiped if their AI tool followed the command. The incident highlights a growing tension between developers who want to control how their open source work is used by AI systems and the risks of taking unilateral, undisclosed action that could harm innocent end users caught in the middle.

title: Apple working to cram massive Gemini model into iPhone to power new Siri
summary: Apple is working to integrate Google's Gemini AI into Siri later this year, but the effort faces significant technical challenges because the full-scale Gemini model is far too large to run entirely on an iPhone. Despite Apple's longstanding emphasis on on-device, privacy-focused AI processing, the new Gemini-powered Siri will likely rely heavily on cloud servers from Google and Nvidia to deliver a capable conversational experience. This marks a notable shift for Apple, which has repeatedly highlighted the privacy benefits of local AI processing as a competitive advantage. The development matters because it signals that even the most resource-rich tech companies cannot yet deliver powerful conversational AI entirely on consumer devices, and it raises fresh questions about user privacy when Siri interactions are routed through third-party cloud infrastructure.

title: Trump loses more control over AI regulation as Illinois passes landmark law
summary: Illinois passed SB 315, the nation's strongest AI safety law, requiring major AI companies to submit public safety plans, undergo independent third-party safety testing, and report critical incidents to the state within 72 hours. The bill comes just days after President Trump canceled a federal plan to vet frontier AI models, effectively pushing regulation down to the state level by default. Notably, both OpenAI and Anthropic supported the legislation, suggesting that large AI firms may prefer a manageable compliance framework over a fragmented mix of varying state laws — and one that raises the bar for smaller competitors who may struggle to meet the same standards. The law matters because it moves AI accountability from voluntary self-reporting toward enforceable public transparency, with independent auditors rather than the companies themselves evaluating safety practices.

title: The AI Hype Index: AI gets booed in graduation season
summary: It is one thing to say AI will change the world. It is another to expect the class of 2026 to applaud it. In fact, when former Google CEO Eric Schmidt told University of Arizona graduates that their task is to help shape AI, he was met with a resounding chorus of boos. “I can…

title: Nvidia bets $150B on Taiwan as Trump's plan to make US an AI hub backfires
summary: Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang announced that the company will invest $150 billion annually in Taiwan, establishing a new headquarters there and reinforcing the island's role as the central hub for AI chip manufacturing and supercomputer production. This commitment represents a dramatic increase from the $10-15 billion Nvidia was spending in Taiwan just a few years ago, and the new facility is expected to be fully operational by 2030. The announcement creates an apparent tension with the Trump administration's push to make the United States the global center of AI development, despite Nvidia previously announcing plans to produce AI chips domestically and projecting up to half a trillion dollars in US AI infrastructure investment. The move underscores a practical reality that many in the tech industry already recognize: Taiwan's deeply embedded manufacturing ecosystem, supply chains, and specialized partners remain extremely difficult to replicate elsewhere in the short or medium term, regardless of political pressure to shift production onshore.

title: YouTube to begin automatically labeling AI videos
summary: YouTube is rolling out a significant update to how it identifies and labels AI-generated videos, moving from a largely voluntary, easy-to-miss system to one that automatically detects AI content using internal signals. Previously, creators were expected to self-report AI use, but labels were buried in video descriptions where most viewers would never notice them. The new system will place labels in prominent, visible locations on both standard videos and YouTube Shorts, and will be triggered automatically when videos show significant photorealistic AI use, contain C2PA metadata confirming AI origin, or were created using Google's own watermarked tools like Veo. This matters because as AI video tools rapidly improve in realism, the ability for viewers to distinguish real footage from AI-generated content is becoming increasingly difficult, making transparent labeling an important step in maintaining trust and informed consumption of online media.

title: US law enforcement warns of "anti-tech extremism" as AI hatred grows
summary: Federal law enforcement agencies, including the FBI and Department of Homeland Security, are now actively monitoring and categorizing opposition to artificial intelligence and technology as a potential domestic extremism threat, according to over 1,000 pages of internal reports obtained by WIRED. The effort introduces a new designation — "anti-tech violent extremism" — that broadly groups together a wide range of critics, protesters, and activists concerned about AI job displacement, data center expansion, and corporate power. This surveillance push aligns with Trump administration directives instructing law enforcement to target individuals holding "anti-capitalism" and "anti-American" beliefs, effectively placing legitimate political dissent under a counterterrorism umbrella. The development raises significant civil liberties concerns, as the government appears to be using national security infrastructure to monitor and potentially criminalize speech and peaceful assembly that challenges the administration's strong financial and political ties to the AI industry.

title: Millions of AI agents imperiled by critical vulnerability in open source package
summary: A critical security vulnerability nicknamed "BadHost" has been discovered in Starlette, an open source Python framework that receives 325 million downloads per week and serves as the foundation for many widely used tools including FastAPI, vLLM, and LiteLLM. The flaw, tracked as CVE-2026-48710, allows attackers to bypass security controls by injecting a single character into an HTTP request header, potentially granting unauthorized access to the servers running it. This is particularly concerning because Starlette underpins many AI agent systems that connect to sensitive external resources — such as databases, email accounts, and calendars — and store the credentials needed to access them, making breached servers an especially rich target. A patch has been released in Starlette version 1.0.1, and organizations running AI infrastructure built on affected frameworks should update immediately, as the vulnerability is described as trivial to exploit on systems without a properly configured firewall.

title: FBI agent explains how easy it is to ID people posting AI porn without consent
summary: The FBI arrested two men under the Take It Down Act, a new law targeting nonconsensual AI-generated sexual imagery, after investigators identified them through relatively straightforward digital detective work. Arturo Hernandez, 20, allegedly created and posted sexualized deepfakes of roughly 50 women — including people he knew personally — and was linked to the content through matching IP addresses, PayPal records, and a saved Instagram photo he had used to generate explicit material. Cornelius Shannon, 51, allegedly published even more content targeting approximately 90 women, and was identified in part because he used his own photo as his profile picture on the porn site. The cases highlight both the serious harm posed by AI-generated nonconsensual imagery and how easily perpetrators can be traced through basic digital footprints like account links, geolocation data, and reused usernames.

title: 3D-printable humanoid legs let robotics experiments run wild
summary: Hugging Face has released the LeRobot Humanoid, an open-source bipedal robot platform starting at $2,500 that researchers and builders can construct using 3D-printed parts and readily available commercial components. The project provides a complete package including assembly instructions, wiring documentation, simulation tools, and control software, making it accessible for real-world AI robotics experimentation without requiring specialized manufacturing. This matters because affordable, reproducible hardware lowers the barrier to entry for robotics research, allowing developers to physically test and refine AI-powered robot behaviors in ways that complement simulation-based training. As part of a broader push to democratize robotics and counter corporate dominance in the field, Hugging Face plans to expand the platform with an upper body and more advanced capabilities, building on earlier open-source efforts like their 3D-printable robotic arm.

title: Rethinking organizational design in the age of agentic AI
summary: Amid rapidly growing adoption of enterprise-level AI agents, there’s a disconnect emerging between ambition and execution. Although 85% of organizations say they want to be agentic within the next three years, 76% say their current operations and infrastructure can’t support that change. They cite a lack of readiness across people, processes, and workflows. The sticky…

summary: Haven’t you heard? White-collar jobs are going away, decimated by AI. Waves of layoffs in the tech sector (most recently at Coinbase and Meta and Cisco) are said to presage what will soon come for all of us knowledge workers. But before you quit your job as a software developer or financial analyst—or tech journalist—and…

title: It’s time to address the looming crisis in entry-level work
summary: Artificial intelligence has not so far produced a clean story of mass unemployment. Aggregate employment in developed countries remains broadly stable, and recent assessments have found limited evidence that AI has shifted the headline numbers. But a troubling change may be hiding beneath the surface: the quiet weakening of the first rung of the career…
